title: Sass: Engineering Professional Stylesheets

questions.question_text: Why do browsers need a compiler for Sass files?
options: Because Sass files are too large
Because Sass uses logic (variables, loops) that the browser doesn't natively understand
To make the files more secure
questions.explanation: Sass is a pre-processor; browsers only understand standard CSS, so the Sass logic must be transformed before deployment.

questions.question_text: In VS Code, which extension is most commonly used to compile Sass in real-time?
questions.explanation: Live Sass Compiler is the de-facto standard for VS Code based Sass development.

questions.question_text: What does the symbol '$' represent in Sass?
questions.explanation: The '$' symbol is used to define and call variables in Sass.

options: A file starting with '_' that is hidden from the compiler and meant to be imported
A file that only contains HTML
questions.explanation: Partials allow you to break code into modules without the compiler creating individual .css files for each one.

questions.question_text: Which directive is used to reuse a block of CSS code defined in a '@mixin'?
questions.explanation: '@include' is the command used to inject a mixin into a selector.

questions.question_text: How do you refer to a parent selector within a nested element?
questions.explanation: The '&' symbol acts as a placeholder for the parent selector.

questions.question_text: What is the result of '$width: 100% / 4' in Sass?
questions.explanation: Sass performs mathematical operations natively during compilation.

questions.question_text: When would you use a '@for' loop in Sass?
options: To fetch data from an API
To automate the creation of repetitive CSS classes (e.g. m-1, m-2, m-3)
questions.explanation: Loops in Sass are used to programmatically generate CSS selectors and values.

questions.question_text: What is the difference between '@extend' and '@mixin'?
options: Mixins are for JavaScript, Extends are for CSS
Mixins inject code into the selector, while Extends group multiple selectors together in the final CSS
questions.explanation: Extending is better for shared properties among many elements, while mixins are better for logic that requires arguments.

questions.question_text: Which Sass function would you use to create a darker version of a color variable for a hover state?
questions.explanation: The darken($color, $percent) function is a built-in Sass helper.

questions.question_text: In Sass architecture, which file name is conventional for the 'main' file that imports all other modules?
questions.explanation: A main.scss (without underscore) is the entry point that the compiler 'watches'.

questions.question_text: How do you define a comment that will NOT appear in the final CSS file?
questions.explanation: Single-line comments (//) are stripped out during the Sass compilation process.

questions.question_text: Which of these is a valid Sass mathematical calculation for a responsive container?
questions.explanation: While Sass handles simple math, native CSS calc() is still used for mixing units like 'vw' and 'px'.

questions.question_text: What does '@each $color in red, blue, green' do?
options: Styles all three colors at once
Iterates through the list of colors to perform a repeatable action for each
Deletes those colors from the theme
Applies all three to the background
questions.explanation: '@each' is used for iterating over lists and maps in Sass.

questions.question_text: Why is 'Live Sass Compiler' better than manual compilation?
options: It makes the CSS more colorful
It automatically generates the CSS every time you save, providing instant feedback
It uploads the code to the server
questions.explanation: Workflows that involve instant 'Watch' mechanisms significantly increase development speed.
